title: The Republic That Wasn't
bullets: On February 12, 1912, the six-year-old Puyi signed an edict of abdication, ending the Qing dynasty and two thousand years of imperial monarchy in one stroke.
Sun Yat-sen gave the revolution its slogan, the Three Principles of the People, and its organization, the Tongmenghui, but was in Denver when the Wuchang Uprising actually toppled the dynasty.
Sun was provisional president of the Republic of China for six weeks before handing power to the empire's strongman, Yuan Shikai, who commanded the modern Beiyang Army.
The Kuomintang won China's first elections under Song Jiaoren, who was shot dead at a Shanghai railway station in 1913 with the trail leading toward Yuan.
Yuan crushed the KMT's Second Revolution, banned the party, dissolved parliament, and ruled as a dictator with a foreign-bankrolled army.
Japan's 1915 Twenty-One Demands would have made China a protectorate; the day Yuan partly accepted became a 'Day of National Humiliation' for a generation.
Yuan declared he would become emperor of a new dynasty in late 1915, misread his own country completely, and the National Protection War broke his regime in months.
Yuan died in June 1916 after eighty-three days as an unrecognized emperor, and the Beiyang Army that had obeyed him personally did not disband — it divided.

title: The Warlord Era
bullets: On paper China had a national government in Beijing with a flag and foreign recognition; in fact it was whichever general's faction currently held the capital.
The Beiyang Army fractured into personal networks — the Zhili clique, the Anhui clique, the Fengtian clique under the ex-bandit Zhang Zuolin in Manchuria.
A warlord's power rested on soldiers, the silver to pay them, and territory to squeeze; a peasant owed taxes to Beijing, a warlord, a garrison, and a landlord at once.
China entered the First World War by sending 140,000 men of the Chinese Labour Corps to dig trenches in France, expecting a seat at the peace table.
The Manchu Restoration of 1917 put eleven-year-old Puyi back on the throne for twelve days before other warlords bombed the Forbidden City from an airplane.
While the countryside collapsed, the treaty ports boomed: Shanghai became one of the largest cities on earth with a new Chinese commercial middle class.
Yan Fu's translations of Huxley and Spencer injected 'survival of the fittest' into Chinese thought as a national life-or-death imperative.
Mission schools and a Westernized student class formed in the cities — the restless young who would make the actual revolution in the next chapter.

title: May Fourth and the New Culture
bullets: The New Culture Movement, led from Chen Duxiu's magazine New Youth, attacked Confucianism itself as the cage holding China down, under the slogans 'Mr. Science' and 'Mr. Democracy'.
Hu Shi led the switch from classical Chinese to vernacular Chinese — the precondition for mass literacy, mass politics, and mass propaganda.
Lu Xun proved the point with savage stories like 'The True Story of Ah Q', becoming modern China's literary conscience.
The 1919 Paris Peace Conference handed Germany's Shandong rights to Japan, and on May 4, 1919, three thousand students marched to Tiananmen.
The May Fourth Movement was the first time street action by students, merchants, and workers reversed a decision of the Chinese state.
The generation split: Hu Shi's liberal road versus the Bolshevik road, which offered both an explanation of imperialism and a vanguard-party technology.
Li Dazhao ran a Marxist study group at Peking University whose lowest-paid assistant, registering newspapers for eight dollars a month, was Mao Zedong.
Comintern agents turned the study circles into the First Congress of the Chinese Communist Party in Shanghai in 1921 — a sect of a dozen men in a country of 400 million.

title: The First United Front and the Northern Expedition
bullets: After thirty years of failed uprisings, the only power that would seriously help Sun Yat-sen was the Soviet Union, and the price was the First United Front with the Communists.
The Sun-Joffe Manifesto and adviser Mikhail Borodin rebuilt the flabby KMT into a disciplined Leninist party with a party army.
The Whampoa Military Academy, run by Chiang Kai-shek with the Communist Zhou Enlai as political director, forged the officer corps of both sides of the later civil war.
Sun Yat-sen died of cancer in 1925; the man with the army, Chiang Kai-shek, won the two-year struggle to inherit the party.
The Northern Expedition launched in 1926 and worked fast because organizers ran ahead building peasant associations and strikes that collapsed warlord rear areas.
Mao Zedong's 1927 Hunan report argued the peasantry, not the urban workers, was the revolution's real engine — the heresy that wins him China twenty years later.
Communist-led workers seized Shanghai for the advancing army; on April 12, 1927, Chiang and Green Gang gunmen answered with a coordinated massacre.
Chiang took Beijing in 1928, won the warlords' nominal submission, and set up a Nationalist government with its capital at Nanjing — reunified on very thin paper.

title: The White Terror and the Nanjing Decade
bullets: The Nanjing Decade built a central bank, a unified currency, and railways while never controlling much of the country it claimed — admirers and critics are both right.
The government was built on the April massacre and never stopped: the White Terror destroyed the Communists' urban organization for years.
Driven from the cities, Mao and the defecting officer Zhu De built the Red Army and a doctrine of trading space for time in the mountains.
By 1931 the scattered bases became the Jiangxi Soviet, a real rural state with land redistribution — and the Futian Incident, an early purge of its own cadres.
Japanese officers staged the 1931 Mukden Incident, overran Manchuria, and installed the ex-emperor Puyi as puppet ruler of Manchukuo.
Chiang's answer was 'first internal pacification, then external resistance' — destroy the Communists before fighting Japan, the most criticized decision of his career.
Four Encirclement Campaigns against the Jiangxi Soviet failed; the fifth, from 1933, used a tightening ring of concrete blockhouses to strangle it.
By autumn 1934 the Soviet was being squeezed to death and the only option left was to break out and run — the Long March.

title: The Long March
bullets: About 86,000 people broke out of Jiangxi in October 1934; roughly a year later somewhere between 4,000 and 8,000 reached the northwest.
At the Xiang River in late 1934 the Red Army lost more than half its strength, discrediting the Soviet-trained leadership that ran the retreat.
The Zunyi Conference of January 1935 is the hinge of the whole book: Mao Zedong won the effective control he would hold until he died in 1976.
The crossing of the Dadu River at Luding Bridge became the central heroic legend — real, but far less heavily opposed than the official story.
Careful historians argue the traditional distance is inflated and the cleanest set pieces are legend hardened around a true core of staggering endurance and death.
Mao's column split from his rival Zhang Guotao, whose larger army was later shattered, leaving Mao unchallenged at the destination of Yan'an.
Students marched in the December 9th Movement of 1935 demanding China stop fighting Communists and start fighting Japan.
In the Xi'an Incident of December 1936, Chiang's own generals kidnapped him and forced a united front — the twist that may have saved the Communist Party.

title: The War of Resistance Begins
bullets: A July 1937 clash at the Marco Polo Bridge escalated into the full Japanese invasion — the War of Resistance Against Japan, eight years and 15 to 20 million Chinese dead.
Chiang threw his best German-trained divisions into the three-month Battle of Shanghai, destroying his modern army but proving China would not collapse quickly.
After Nanjing fell, the Nanjing Massacre killed an estimated 200,000 to 300,000 — a figure contested by Japanese revisionists but the atrocity itself is not in doubt.
The government retreated to Chongqing and breached the Yellow River dikes in 1938, drowning hundreds of thousands of its own people to slow Japan.
The Communists fought a different war: the Eighth Route Army spread into the countryside, built base areas, and cut rents and interest instead of seizing land.
Party membership went from about 40,000 in 1937 to well over a million by 1945 — the war transferred the loyalty of tens of millions of rural Chinese.
The one big conventional Communist push, the 1940 Hundred Regiments Offensive, drew a devastating 'kill all, burn all' reprisal and a return to guerrilla strategy.
The New Fourth Army Incident of 1941 cracked the united front in the open; the two 'allies' barely cooperated again.

title: The Long War and the Two Chinas
bullets: Pearl Harbor made China a formal Allied power; the 1943 Cairo Conference abolished the unequal treaties and promised great-power status.
The Stilwell crisis of 1944 poisoned the American view of the Nationalists exactly when American opinion mattered for the postwar settlement.
Printing money to fund the war produced a hyperinflation that destroyed the salaried middle class, the bureaucracy, and the officer corps the state ran on.
The 1942–43 Henan Famine killed two to three million while officials covered it up and the army kept requisitioning grain — running alongside the Bengal Famine.
In Yan'an, Mao ran the Yan'an Rectification Movement — study, confession, group criticism, forced confessions — inventing the template for the mass campaigns to come.
The 1945 Seventh Congress wrote Mao Zedong Thought into the party constitution; the party that would rule China was now ideologically finished.
Japan's 1944 Operation Ichigo smashed Nationalist armies across central and southern China, exposing how badly the regime had decayed.
When Japan surrendered, a Soviet blitz overran Manchuria and handed its arsenals and a strategic base to the Communists.

title: The Civil War and 1949
bullets: China won the longest war in its modern history in 1945 and was in an all-out civil war by the summer of 1946.
The Marshall Mission failed because two Leninist party-armies would never voluntarily merge into one neutral national army — unlike India's independence struggle.
The Nationalists held the cities and even captured Yan'an in 1947, but holding points in a peasant country meant controlling nothing in between.
The 1947 radical land reform converted the rural majority into a recruitment and supply base no Chinese army had ever commanded.
Lin Biao destroyed the Nationalists' best armies in the Liaoshen Campaign; the million-man Huaihai Campaign annihilated their central forces.
A catastrophic 1948 gold yuan reform wiped out the last urban savings the wartime inflation had left.
The PLA crossed the Yangtze in April 1949; Chiang's government, two million followers, the gold reserve, and the imperial art collection withdrew to Taiwan.
On October 1, 1949, Mao proclaimed the People's Republic of China from Tiananmen — 'the Chinese people have stood up'.

title: New China: Land Reform and Korea
bullets: For hundreds of millions the key fact of 1950 was that the wars had stopped and one government ran the whole country — the regime's real early legitimacy.
The provisional Common Program promised a moderate New Democracy; the transformation under it was anything but moderate.
Nationwide land reform moved about 40 percent of cultivated land through violent 'speak bitterness' meetings, killing several hundred thousand to over a million.
The Marriage Law of 1950 banned arranged marriage and concubinage and gave women divorce rights — one of the revolution's most consequential changes.
The PLA entered Xinjiang and Tibet; the 1951 Seventeen Point Agreement was signed under military pressure by the young Dalai Lama's representatives.
Mao sent hundreds of thousands of 'Chinese People's Volunteers' into the Korean War and fought the strongest country on earth to a stalemate.
The 'Resist America, Aid Korea' mobilization justified the Campaign to Suppress Counterrevolutionaries and the Three-Anti and Five-Anti campaigns.
By 1953 the country was unified, mobilized, and frightened of its government, and Mao turned to how fast China could be made socialist.

title: From the First Plan to the Hundred Flowers
bullets: The First Five-Year Plan and the Soviet 156 projects built the heavy-industrial core of the economy almost from nothing — the one period the plan delivered.
The 1954 Constitution created the formal state structure under unchanging party supremacy; the Gao Gang purge showed losing a top fight was lethal.
Collectivization took peasants' land back within five years of land reform handing it to them, with Mao personally driving the 1955 'high tide'.
The Socialist Transformation absorbed nearly all private firms by 1956, ending the mixed economy the Common Program had promised.
Simplified Chinese characters, a standard spoken language, and pinyin were genuine, largely successful pushes for mass literacy.
Khrushchev secretly denounced Stalin in 1956, unsettling Mao and pushing him toward a distinctively Chinese road.
The Hundred Flowers Campaign invited intellectuals to criticize the party; whether it was a trap from the start is a genuine unresolved debate.
The 1957 Anti-Rightist Campaign purged 550,000-plus intellectuals — run by Deng Xiaoping — silencing exactly the people who could have warned Mao.

title: The Great Leap Forward
bullets: Mao launched the Great Leap Forward to overtake Britain and reach communism by sheer mobilized will — and produced the largest famine in human history.
The countryside was reorganized into giant People's Communes that pooled land, labor, tools, and even cooking, abolishing private life.
Tens of millions melted pots and tools in backyard furnaces to hit steel quotas while the harvest rotted in the fields.
Officials reported wildly inflated 'sputnik fields' yields; the state took grain as a share of the fake figures, leaving villages with nothing.
The Four Pests Campaign exterminated so many sparrows that insects ate the crops — an ecological backfire at national scale.
At the 1959 Lushan Conference, Peng Dehuai privately criticized the Leap and Mao destroyed him, ensuring no official dared report the famine.
After Lushan the procurement machine ground on for two more years against a collapsing harvest.
Unlike India, a country of hundreds of millions had no mechanism by which anyone could tell the leader he was wrong.

title: The Great Famine and the Retreat
bullets: The Great Chinese Famine is the deadliest in human history; honest scholars disagree by a factor of three, most estimates falling between 30 and 45 million dead.
The Xinyang Incident is the famine in miniature: about a million died in one Henan prefecture whose granaries held grain the whole time.
Liu Shaoqi and Deng Xiaoping took over economic policy, dismantled the worst communes, restored private plots, and pulled the country back from the edge.
At the 1962 Seven Thousand Cadres Conference Liu said the disaster was 'three parts natural, seven parts man-made' to Mao's face.
The Sino-Soviet split became open in 1960 when Moscow withdrew all its advisers, leaving hundreds of projects half-built mid-famine.
China fought and won the short 1962 Sino-Indian War even as its own people starved — a juxtaposition that captures the regime.
Mao never forgave the men proven right at his expense and prepared an attack on the party itself through the Socialist Education Movement.
A coded feud over the play Hai Rui Dismissed from Office was the opening shot of the Cultural Revolution.

title: The Cultural Revolution Unleashed
bullets: Mao turned the population loose to destroy the party and state he himself had built — sincere conviction and a bid to crush his rivals, inseparably both.
Lin Biao had turned the army into a machine of Mao-worship and produced the Little Red Book, the physical totem of the cult.
The 1966 May 16 Circular and a radical group including Jiang Qing took the campaign over the normal party structure.
Red Guards wrote big-character posters and smashed the 'Four Olds' — temples, art, libraries — while attacking teachers and officials; schools closed for years.
Liu Shaoqi, head of state and the man who told the truth about the famine, was beaten and left to die in a prison cellar in 1969.
Rival Red Guard factions fought with escalating weapons until the 1967 Wuhan Incident nearly tipped China into civil war and the army was sent in.
The Down to the Countryside Movement shipped some 17 million urban youth to villages — China's lost generation, one of them a future leader named Xi Jinping.
The deadliest phase was the quiet Cleansing of the Class Ranks of 1968–69, killing on the order of a million with far less public drama.

title: The Long Twilight
bullets: Border battles with the USSR at Zhenbao Island in 1969 brought the two Communist powers near war and pushed China toward an opening to the United States.
Lin Biao, the constitutional successor, allegedly plotted a coup and died fleeing in a 1971 plane crash — the official account, with key facts still disputed.
The Lin Biao affair is where mass belief in Maoism quietly broke: if Mao's closest comrade was a traitor, the whole sacred narrative was a lie.
Ping-pong diplomacy led to President Nixon's visit to China in 1972; the PRC took the China seat at the United Nations in 1971.
The deathbed succession fight pitted Jiang Qing's Gang of Four against the rebuilders around the dying Zhou Enlai.
Zhou maneuvered Deng Xiaoping back to run the government and push the Four Modernizations before the radicals purged Deng a second time.
Zhou died in January 1976; mass mourning became the Tiananmen Incident; the Tangshan earthquake killed a quarter-million as Mao lay dying.
Mao Zedong died on September 9, 1976; within a month Hua Guofeng arrested the Gang of Four, ending the Cultural Revolution by force.
